summary: The article supports one procedural mechanism for why keyword filtering failed, while the main outcome claims remain descriptive and must stay explicitly bounded.

bounded_claims.claim_text: The final performance-related pull request set was distributed across many repositories, agents, and software-stack areas rather than concentrated in a narrow optimization niche.
bounded_claims.bounds: The evidence supports breadth of distribution in this dataset, but not the mechanism behind that spread; it does not show whether the breadth reflects underlying optimization demand, agent behavior, repository composition, or topic-model grouping choices.

bounded_claims.claim_text: Performance-related pull requests were rejected more often than non-performance pull requests in the studied agentic development data.
bounded_claims.bounds: The evidence establishes an outcome difference only; it does not identify whether the higher rejection rate comes from technical difficulty, stricter review standards, deployment risk, or repository-specific governance.

bounded_claims.claim_text: Among accepted performance-related pull requests, merge times varied by category, with UI, AI, and Analytics taking longest and Low-level work merging fastest, while change size showed only weak association with merge time.
bounded_claims.bounds: The evidence supports category-level merge-time differences and weak size association in this dataset, but it does not justify a causal account of why some categories merged slower; domain complexity, review burden, and risk remain unresolved possibilities.
